subtopic: Finance, Money and Private Member Bills
title: Finance, Money and Private Member Bills
focus: Compares Finance and Money Bills and explains the mechanism for Private Member Bills.
rationale: Fallback section derived from packet-level grouping.
sectionExcerpt:
[Page 015]
Article 117(3): Bills involving expenditure from Consolidated Fund of India require prior President’s recommendation.
About Finance Bill and Money Bill
- Finance Bill can be introduced only in Lok Sabha. Rajya Sabha can only recommend amendments in Bill, it cannot amend or reject.
- Bill has to be passed by Parliament within 75 days of its introduction.
- As a finance bill is a money bill so no joint sitting of two houses is allowed with regard to a finance bill under Article 108.
- Article 109 mentions special procedure in respect of Money Bills.
- If Lok Sabha does not accept any Rajya Sabha recommendations, Money Bill is deemed to be passed by both Houses in the form passed by Lok Sabha without any amendments recommended by Rajya Sabha.
1.10.2. PRIVATE MEMBER BILL
- About: A MP, other than a Minister (both elected and nominated) is known as a Private Member. A bill initiated by any such Member is called a Private Member's Bill (PMB).
- Drafting of such a bill is responsibility of the Member introducing it.
- Mechanism: One month notice is given before bill’s introduction with permission of Speaker (Lok Sabha) or Chairman (Rajya Sabha). If the bill is selected then:
- Lok Sabha allots last two-and-a-half hours of business every Friday for consideration and discussion on the Bill.
- Rajya Sabha allots two and a half hours (from 2.30 p.m. to 5.00 p.m.) on every alternate Friday.
- First private member bill passed was Muslim Wakfs Bill, 1952 which was introduced by Syed Mohammed Ahmad Kasmi.
- Only 14 private member bills have become law till date and the last such bill was passed in 1970.
1.10.3. ADJOURNMENT SINE DIE
- Sine Die is a Latin term meaning "without assigning a day for a further meeting."
- In parliamentary terms, it refers to end of a session of Parliament without setting a date to reconvene.
- Authority: Presiding Officer announces and later, President issues notification to prorogue House, officially ending the session (Article 85).
- Effect on Pending Business:
- All bills that are pending in the same House remain unaffected.
- Most notices (e.g., questions, motions, resolutions) lapse and must be resubmitted in the next session unless otherwise s...
